name: ad-hoc
context: Run one-off Ansible modules across selected hosts without creating a full playbook
info: Ad-hoc commands are the fastest execution surface in Ansible: they use the `ansible` CLI to run a single module against one or more inventory targets, with support for parallel forks, become, check mode, and inventory patterns. Agents use this skill for investigative work, emergency fixes, fact gathering, service or package changes, file transfer, and other rare tasks that are not worth promoting into a reusable playbook yet. The critical guardrails are module choice, shell quoting, inventory targeting, and explicit limits, because ad-hoc commands are quick but intentionally not reusable.
triggers: install a package with ansible cli
use check mode in ansible cli
limit an ansible command to one group
body:
# Ansible Ad-Hoc Commands

Use the `ansible` command when you need one module, one target pattern, and a quick answer. It is ideal for rare or investigative operations; if the task needs to be repeated, reviewed, or staged, promote it into [[ansible/playbooks]].

## Common Examples

```bash
ansible all -i inventory.yml -m ansible.builtin.ping
ansible web -i inventory.yml -b -m ansible.builtin.package -a 'name=nginx state=present'
ansible web -i inventory.yml -b -m ansible.builtin.service -a 'name=nginx state=restarted'
ansible db -i inventory.yml -m ansible.builtin.command -a 'systemctl status postgresql'
ansible all -i inventory.yml -m ansible.builtin.setup -a 'filter=ansible_distribution*'
```

## Check Mode and Limits

```bash
ansible web -i inventory.yml --limit canary --check -m ansible.builtin.file -a 'path=/etc/motd state=touch mode=0644'
```

## command vs shell

Prefer declarative modules such as `package`, `service`, `copy`, `template`, and `user`. If you need shell features like pipes or redirects, switch from `ansible.builtin.command` to `ansible.builtin.shell` deliberately and quote carefully so the local shell does not expand variables before Ansible sends the command.

## Parallelism and Privilege

`-f` controls fork count, `-u` sets the remote user, and `-b` enables privilege escalation. Those flags are operationally important during fleet changes and emergency fixes.

sections.title: Prefer modules over shell commands
sections.body: The most reliable ad-hoc runs use purpose-built modules such as `ansible.builtin.package`, `ansible.builtin.service`, `ansible.builtin.copy`, `ansible.builtin.file`, and `ansible.builtin.user`. They are more declarative and more idempotent than arbitrary shell. Use `command` for plain commands and `shell` only when you actually need shell syntax like pipes, redirects, or globbing.

sections.title: Patterns and limits prevent full-fleet mistakes
sections.body: The first argument to `ansible` is a host pattern, so inventory quality directly determines blast radius. Use patterns such as `web`, `db:&prod`, or `all`, and apply `--limit` for an even tighter subset such as a canary host. Validate inventory with [[ansible/inventory]] before a disruptive run.

sections.title: Remote user, SSH, and become
sections.body: Use `-u` to connect as a specific user, `--private-key` to select an SSH key, and `-b` or `--become-user` for privilege escalation. Many ad-hoc failures are really SSH or sudo issues, not module issues. Load [[ssh/connect]] and [[ssh/config]] when connection behavior is inconsistent across hosts.

sections.title: Check mode for safe previews
sections.body: Use `--check` to ask supported modules what they would change without applying it. This is especially useful before a package or file change on a large host group. Not every module implements check mode equally well, so combine it with targeted host limits and verbose output when stakes are high.

links.context: Inventory selection is the first safety check for any ad-hoc run

related.note: Ad-hoc commands rely on correctly resolved inventory and patterns
Ad-hoc tasks are the simplest form of execution; playbooks generalize them
Most Linux-host execution depends on working SSH access

scripts.name: Run Ad-Hoc Command
scripts.description: Execute a single Ansible module against an inventory pattern with optional become, limits, and check mode

ANSIBLE_PATTERN.description: Inventory pattern to target, such as all, web, or db:&prod

ANSIBLE_INVENTORY_SOURCE.description: Inventory file, directory, or plugin source

ANSIBLE_MODULE_ARGS.description: Module arguments in key=value or JSON form

ANSIBLE_BECOME.description: Set to true to enable privilege escalation

ANSIBLE_CHECK_MODE.description: Set to true to preview supported module changes

ANSIBLE_REMOTE_USER.description: Remote SSH user for the ansible CLI

ANSIBLE_PRIVATE_KEY_FILE.description: Path to the SSH private key file

ANSIBLE_LIMIT.description: Extra host limit applied after pattern matching

ANSIBLE_EXTRA_VARS.description: Extra vars passed with -e in key=value or JSON form

ANSIBLE_ONE_LINE.description: Set to true to enable one-line output with -o

inventorySource.description: Inventory file, directory, or plugin source

moduleArgs.description: Module arguments in key=value or JSON form

check.description: Preview supported changes without applying them

command.description: The ansible CLI invocation that was executed

depends.reason: The ansible CLI targets hosts selected from inventory
Most managed hosts are reached over SSH
